Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional because I had to work around so many constraints. I would grab study time whenever I could - early mornings before anyone else was up, late nights, weekends. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often practice problems physically, write things out multiple times, and create my own examples rather than just reading. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access internet and download what I needed in advance.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on what was due and what was happening at home. Some weeks I could manage a steady 14 hours spread out, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Family responsibilities and financial pressures meant I couldn't always stick to an ideal schedule. I learned to be flexible and make the most of whatever time windows opened up for me.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew there was something specific I needed - checking for new assignments, downloading materials, or seeing if grades were posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I wasn't missing any announcements since I couldn't always attend classes. Fear of falling behind was honestly a big motivator for me to check in, even when I was stressed about what I might find there.
Q4: It never really became routine for me, to be honest. My login pattern was more reactive than habitual - I'd go in when I needed something specific or when anxiety about missing something pushed me to check. With my limited internet access, I had to be intentional about each login rather than casually browsing. I wished I could have made it more of a daily habit, but my circumstances didn't really allow for that consistency.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me - I watched over 168 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. Since my attendance was so low due to various circumstances, these videos were my lifeline. I also found the discussion posts helpful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives and helped me check my understanding.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and optional practice modules because I had to prioritize ruthlessly. With limited time and internet access, I focused on what would directly impact my grades. Some of the interactive elements required consistent internet connection which I couldn't always maintain, so those got pushed aside. I felt guilty about it, but survival mode meant making hard choices.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam preparation or assignment completion. I needed to feel like I was building toward something concrete, not just consuming content passively. As a kinesthetic learner, activities that let me actually do something - solve problems, apply concepts - felt much more valuable than just reading or watching. If I couldn't see the practical payoff, it was hard to justify the time investment.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required work got my attention because the stakes were clear - my grades depended on it. With optional stuff, I always felt this tension between wanting to learn more deeply and knowing I had to protect my limited time and energy for what counted. Sometimes I felt scared that skipping optional content would come back to hurt me on exams.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I would download PDFs, lecture slides, and videos when I had internet access, then study from those materials for days without logging back in. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes and practice problems. I also used some free textbooks I found and borrowed materials from classmates. The LMS was really just my source for getting materials, not where my actual learning happened.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course content but completely invisible online. I'd spend hours working through problems by hand, teaching concepts to myself out loud, or creating physical flashcards and diagrams. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts. My best learning happened away from screens, using tactile methods that suited how my brain works.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd have one focused session where I'd download everything I might need for the next week or two, then work through it all offline. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes, then review those notes repeatedly without the video. It saved data and meant I could study during times when internet wasn't available. My exam score reflects learning that happened mostly disconnected from the platform.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear almost paralyzed me. When I saw how far behind I was on assignments or realized my attendance was so low, I'd feel this wave of panic. Financial stress at home sometimes made it hard to focus on academics at all. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt scary because I didn't want to see what I was missing. The CBT sessions actually helped me work through some of that fear.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were probably my biggest challenge. I had family obligations and limited resources that meant academics couldn't always come first, even when I wanted them to. Fatigue was real - studying after long days meant my brain wasn't always at its best. When I was overwhelmed, I'd sometimes just download materials without really engaging, planning to come back when I had more mental capacity.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd be scrolling through the LMS or watching a video, but my mind was somewhere else entirely - worrying about things at home or just exhausted. There were times I'd technically complete a reading but couldn't tell you what it said. I learned to recognize when I was just going through motions versus actually learning, and I'd try to stop and come back when I could be more present.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was fear - I was scared of not doing well, so avoiding the task felt safer temporarily. Sometimes I genuinely didn't have the time or resources to do them properly, so I'd wait hoping circumstances would improve. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this, but I tried to make up for it by really focusing on exam preparation.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of activity for me. I'd be relatively quiet on the LMS, then suddenly very active as a deadline approached. This wasn't ideal, but it was how I managed with everything else going on. Deadlines gave me permission to prioritize academics over other demands - I could tell myself and others that I had no choice. They were stressful but also motivating in a way.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me because it helped me know if my independent studying was actually working. When I got good feedback, it boosted my confidence and made me feel like my approach was valid despite low attendance. Negative feedback was harder - it would trigger my anxiety and sometimes make me avoid that area for a while. I tried to use feedback constructively, but emotionally it wasn't always easy.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having materials available online was essential for someone like me who couldn't attend regularly. But the pacing assumed consistent engagement which didn't match my reality. I appreciated that exams weighted heavily because that's where I could show what I'd actually learned. The structure rewarded outcomes over process, which worked in my favor.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made everything possible for me - that was the biggest help. The discussion forums were good because I could participate asynchronously. What made things harder was when activities required real-time participation or consistent internet access. Also, some of the interactive elements didn't work well on my older phone, which was often my only way to access things.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something - maybe they're confused about what's important, or they're anxious and checking repeatedly without actually doing the work. It could also be that they're engaged with the content but struggling with the actual assignments due to difficulty or life circumstances. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting as clicking around.
Q21: This is literally my experience. My LMS activity was low, but I earned a Distinction because my real learning happened offline. I downloaded materials efficiently, studied intensively on my own, and performed well on exams. The platform couldn't see my notebooks full of practice problems or the hours I spent working through concepts. Low engagement metrics don't mean low engagement with learning - they might just mean different methods.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how long they were technically connected. They can track submission patterns and participation in online discussions. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just time spent, emotional state while learning, and whether someone actually understood the material. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I watch videos - it's about whether I'm actively thinking, questioning, and connecting new information to what I already know. For me, engagement often looks like writing, practicing, and sometimes struggling alone with a concept until it clicks. It's internal and effortful.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how scared I felt sometimes, or how hard I worked to overcome obstacles that had nothing to do with academics. It wouldn't capture the resilience it took to keep going when things were difficult at home, or the satisfaction of understanding something after struggling with it alone. It wouldn't show that my low attendance wasn't apathy - it was circumstances. My journey was so much more than what any log could capture.
